entry.title: Announcing the New "In-Metro" Disaster Recovery Solution for SAP BTP
entry.content:
Overview

We’re excited to unveil the latest enhancement to SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP):
The In-Metro Disaster Recovery (DR) solution.

This solution is designed to protect you against disasters affecting a single Availability Zone (AZ) with contractually committed SLAs RPO and RTO, offering enhanced resilience and uninterrupted operations for your cloud services.

 

What is "In-Metro" DR for SAP BTP?

The In-Metro DR solution is built to protect against local disasters by leveraging a Multi-AZ setup. It ensures that, in the event of a single AZ disaster, which is defined by SAP, services remain operational, data is protected, and business continues without significant downtime.

The key metrics are:

  • RPO (Recovery Point Objective): 5 minutes – Ensuring that data can be recovered with a maximum of 5 minutes' loss in case of a disaster.
  • RTO (Recovery Time Objective): 2 hours – Ensuring that the full system is restored and operational within 2 hours of an incident.

 

The Power of Multi-AZ Architecture

At the heart of In-Metro DR is the Multi-AZ (Multi Availability Zone) architecture, ensuring that your applications and databases are spread across different zones to safeguard against single-point failures.

This includes:

  • Active-active applications across 3 AZs: Applications are distributed across three AZs, with real-time data replication ensuring that if one AZ fails, the others automatically take over without impacting availability.
  • Active-passive databases across 2 AZs: Databases are set up in active-passive mode, with a failover mechanism in place to switch operations to a backup AZ if the primary one fails.

Through automatic failover, load balancing, and failure detection, the solution ensures that services stay online seamlessly. Internal stress tests, such as regular “Chaos Day” exercises, further reinforce the reliability of this DR solution.

 

Availability and Scope

 As of now, the In-Metro DR solution is not available for all SAP BTP services. While it already covers a wide range of services, its scope will continue to expand over time as we improve coverage and extend it to additional SAP BTP services. The services in scope will be regularly updated in the corresponding documents.

In-Metro DR is specifically designed for SAP-managed scenarios, meaning that customers using SAP BTP under SAP’s management can take advantage of the In-Metro DR setup without additional configuration or costs. However, for customer-managed scenarios, such as side-by-side extensions, the customer is responsible for configuring it accordingly (e.g. deploy the apps with multiple instances or configure data replicas on the persistency level).

 

 DR Planning and Regular Testing

 To ensure the highest levels of reliability:

  • The DR plan is updated every 12 months, ensuring that it incorporates the latest technology and best practices.
  • A DR test is conducted at least once a year to verify that the solution can meet its RPO and RTO commitments under real-world conditions.

The In-Metro DR solution currently supports SAP BTP regions hosted on both AWS and Azure cloud infrastructures, providing flexibility for your organization’s cloud setup.

 

 Next Steps

 By leveraging In-Metro DR within SAP-managed scenarios, you can trust that your critical data and services are protected with cutting-edge resilience, without the need for extra investment. For customer-managed scenarios, setting up corresponding HA and DR solutions will ensure your setup is equally resilient.

To explore which services are already covered and get all information, check the description in SAP Trust Center.

Alternatively, you can watch a deep dive into this new solution at SAP TechED: Watch the SAP TechED session on In-Metro DR.

entry.title: Shape the Future: Join the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Cycle 3, 2024!
entry.content:
We're excited to announce that the third cycle of the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative for 2024 has begun, featuring 33 new projects designed to enhance innovation and collaboration for our valued SAP customers and partners.

In today's rapidly evolving business environment, staying ahead is crucial. The SAP Customer Engagement Initiative offers you a unique chance to gain early insights into SAP's product developments. By participating, you'll collaborate directly with our developers and product managers, influencing the future direction of our products.

Act quickly! Registration for the projects is open until November 15, 2024. Don’t miss this chance to make your voice heard and shape the next generation of SAP solutions.

To assist you in exploring the projects, we’ve created a detailed list for your convenience. Check out the full list here or follow the links below to discover the opportunities available:

SAP S/4HANA & SAP S/4HANA Cloud

  • Composable Enterprise: Reimagining Enterprise Software for Unprecedented Business Agility 
  • Help Where and When You Need It: User Assistance in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • Improve your Post Upgrade Tests(PUT) Experience - An Integrated and Smart approach to PUT
  • Insightful Analytics for Actual Costing
  • Joule applications to SAP S/4HANA Utilities
  • Monitoring End to End Master Data Replications in SAP Cloud ALM
  • New Cost Settlement for real estate scenarios (Service Charge Settlement / CAM)
  • Purpose as base for processing of S/4HANA Business Partner personal data – concept and migration”
  • Research Project: Production Scheduling using AI
  • Research: SAP S/4HANA migration cockpit - AI: Field Mapping Proposal
  • Using AI to Extract Data from the Delivery Note to Update the Inbound Delivery

SAP Business Technology Platform

  • AI Agent Builder - A Low-Code Innovation with SAP Build
  • AI-Powered Autonomous Sandbox
  • Cloud Security: Improving the Encryption Key Management Experience
  • Discover SAP Build Process Automation Triggers: Empowering Experts to Automate and Extend Use Cases
  • GenAI Content Provenance and Authenticity via Watermarking
  • Investigation into a Customer Security Dashboard
  • Knowledge Graph RAG on unstructured Data

SAP Concur

  • Concur Expense - India GST handling
  • Concur Expense – Integrating Sales Opportunity Data with Concur Travel and Expense Data
  • Concur Expense - Preventing overspend through cost control, spend planning, and regulatory
  • Concur Expense - SAP Integration with Concur Solutions: Employee- and Benefit- focused Budget Management
  • Concur Expense – The Future of Expense Administration
  • Concur Travel - Trip Management / New Trip List
  • SAP Concur Solutions - Manage and track carbon consumption in T&E spend

Customer Experience

  • Redesigned SAP Sales Cloud, add-in for Microsoft Outlook

Data & Analytics

  • SAP HANA Database Migration to SAP HANA Cloud (XSC,XSA,CAP)

SAP Globalization

  • AI-supported Extension of Electronic Document Processing of SAP Document and Reporting Compliance
  • Localization as a Self-Service for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition with Business AI
  • Sales and Use Tax Reporting in United States - User Research

Lifecycle Management

  • Business Service Management in SAP Cloud ALM operations
  • Clean Core / RISE with SAP Methodology Dashboard in SAP Cloud ALM

Others

  • Early Talent Management with SAP SuccessFactors

We believe that collaboration is essential for driving innovation. By partnering with our customers and partners, we can collectively shape the future of SAP’s products and revolutionize business operations.

So, mark your calendars, rally your team, and let’s embark on this journey together. Together, we can foster progress, and transform industries.

Don’t wait—register now at influence.sap.com and join the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Cycle 3 in 2024!

Warm regards,

Your SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Team

Follow-up after registration: When you register for a project, you will be invited to an introductory call with the SAP project lead. At this point further participation is optional. Typically, all activities are governed by the Feedback Agreement with SAP.

entry.title: Reminder: SAP Data and Analytics Forum 12.-13.11.2024
entry.content:
We look forward to welcoming you on the 12th to 13th of November at this year’s German-speaking SAP Data and Analytics Forum.  

In addition to the on-site event at the SAP Training Center in Walldorf, which will take place on both days, this blog will focus on the virtual event, giving you the flexibility to participate from anywhere. 

The first day is free and virtual. Current trends and developments in Data and Analytics are discussed here. From best practices to new technologies and applications such as SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Datasphere, you will be able to gain valuable insights and expand your knowledge.   

Anticipate three parallel tracks:   

  1. SAP Analytics Cloud: Strategy & Roadmap SAP Analytics Cloud   
  2. SAP Datasphere: Strategy & Roadmap SAP Datasphere  
  3. Extended Planning & Analytics: Potentials and insights from practical projects.  

Sign up here for the first free and virtual day (preliminary agenda)   

SAP Product Learning CoE Analytics

entry.title: Get Hands-On: Build an Intelligent Data Application powered by the SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine
entry.content:
With the release of SAP HANA Cloud's Vector Engine, our development team built an exciting Movie Insight Demo App, which showcase the engine's powerful vector-based querying and storage capabilities. This demo illustrates the vector engine’s role in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), enhancing LLM responses with contextual data. Now, the entire demo is available on GitHub, providing a hands-on experience for developers & tech enthusiasts to explore the Vector Engine capabilities and practical application.

Before getting into the details, I want to give a shoutout to our awesome development team: Igor Pessoa Rocha, Wenzel Svojanovsky, Wanping Chen and Christian Schuetz for their contribution in building this demo from scratch.

What Does the Demo App Offer?

The Movie Insight Demo App enables users to understand how vector embeddings enhance Large Language Models (LLMs) by providing context-aware responses. The demo illustrates a Retrieval-Augmented Generation workflow, where additional context is retrieved via embedding vectors before a request is sent to the LLM. The similarity between two vectors is calculated through cosine similarity & L2Distance functions, which measures the semantic likeness of two texts, even across different languages.

Reference Architecture

Key Features:

  • Multiple Scenarios: Allows users to explore movie datasets from 1977 to 2024.
  • RAG Mode: Toggle between direct LLM requests or context-augmented queries using vector embeddings.
  • Expert and Standard Views: Offers a simplified view for beginners or an expert mode for advanced users, showing process steps and allowing custom selections.
  • Interactive Elements: Visualize process durations, browse movie datasets, and customize LLM prompts.

The Movie Insight App - An Intelligent Data Application

Role of SAP HANA Cloud vecor engine in the app:

In this demo scenario, the movie titles, their release years, and plots are all stored in SAP HANA Cloud, with the plots being converted & stored as vector embeddings. These embeddings are crucial for providing contextual information to the LLM, by storing data as vectors, the vector engine enhances the LLM’s ability to understand and respond to user queries with context, thus improving accuracy and relevance.

Get Started

To try out the Movie Insight Demo App, check out the full repository on GitHub. The code provides everything needed to build an intelligent data app using SAP HANA Cloud’s Vector Engine. Whether you are a developer looking to understand retrieval-augmented generation or want to explore how cosine similarity can enrich similarity search results, this demo app offers an in-depth, hands-on experience.

If you would like to start using the SAP HANA Cloud vector engine, you can spin up a free trial instance of SAP HANA Cloud in SAP BTP Cockpit by following the steps in this tutorial.

entry.title: Addressing Dictionary Issues with OSTR-Replicated Tables in SAP HANA Scale-Out Systems
entry.content:
Assumption: You Have Scaled Out SAP S/4HANA

Understanding Synchronous Table Replication

What and Why?

In a scale-out system, tables (or selected columns of column store tables) may be replicated across multiple hosts. This replication is essential to reduce network traffic, especially when frequently changing master data must be joined with other tables or partitions on different hosts.

Types of Table Replication

Synchronous vs. Asynchronous

Both asynchronous and synchronous table replication have their advantages and limitations. Below is a comparison of these two approaches:

Feature Optimistic Synchronous Table Replication (OSTR) Asynchronous Table Replication (ATR)
Advantages- Always consistent state between source and replica.
- No need for application developers to modify queries.
- Load balancing without changes to SQL.		- Lower overhead at the source node.
- Less impact on source transactions.		Disadvantages- Performance penalty during write transactions.- Asymmetry leads to potential outdated data on replicas.
- Developers must manage which queries can accept staleness.

For detailed insights, refer to the official SAP documentation here.

Recent Limitations and Fixes

Due to certain limitations, OSTR replication had to be disabled for specific tables during transport or structural changes. Users have encountered the following symptoms:

Error Message:

 

 
DBSL error 99 (db code 7): feature not supported: cannot perform statement when DDL AUTOCOMMIT OFF and table replication is turned on.

Solution

Recent SAP notes have addressed these issues. To ensure proper handling of DDL operations for tables with OSTR replication, follow these steps:

  1. Pre-requisites: Ensure you have the kernel patch provided with SAP Note 3456925 (DBSL procedure HDB_DBSL_CHECK_SCALEOUT).

  2. Implementation Steps:

    • Step 1: Stop the Application Server and back up the Kernel directory.
    • Step 2: Extract the LIB_DBSL.SAR file (as recommended in SAP Note 3456925).
    • Step 3: Restart the Application Server.
    • Step 4: Implement SAP Note 3486167 for the necessary corrections.

      By following these steps, you can successfully manage OSTR replication in your SAP S/4HANA scale-out environment, mitigating errors and ensuring smooth operations during table modifications.

entry.title: Unlocking OptML potential via user-defined functions for Business Uplift
entry.content:
Introduction

In many business areas the users need to find the right set of parameters or business tactics to ensure they can achieve their targets. This problem is resolved in many cases by trial and error by the very experienced users and relies on their ability to forecast or project how the changes they make have an impact on their bottom-lines. For a large part the problem of forecasting can be and is usually solved via Machine Learning trained on their historic data. However, in cases when you need to first provide the right (or "optimal") input to achieve your optimal target the problem is only partially solved. The business users still need to figure out the right set of inputs to provide to the machine learning model to get the desired forecast.

The technique described here has much broader applicability but if you are like me, it helps to see it applied for a concrete business problem. In this blog post we will address the problem by applying it to the real world problem that occurs in Trade Promotion Optimization for Consumer Goods industry. In this context we need to find the right price reduction tactics to ensure the optimal uplift in volumes and other derived targets like  net sales, profitability and ROI.

The focus of this blog post is the use of black box optimization techniques when the predicted uplift is defined by inferring from a pre-trained machine learning model. The machine learning model itself has been trained on customer specific data and understands the price-volume elasticity or in this case specifically the price reduction volume uplift elasticity.

The optimization model provides decision support to the business user(account managers in this chosen context) by exploring the space of different price points and temporary price reduction tactics like discount percentages vs absolute dollars off, Buy 1 Get 1,  Multi-Buy etc. All of these plus the communication  and display mechanisms have a significant impact on the projected uplifts even when the financial incentive value is the same to the end user. For example consumer behaviour is very different if they see a promotion of 2$ off on a product normally priced at 20$ off vs 10% off. Also the impact is influenced by whether the end user receives the communication of the promotion via a front-page ad(typically more expensive for the business but produces a much higher uplift) vs a coupon page or in-store communication when the consumer enters the outlet.

The ML infused optimization model, hence OptML, is deployed and served via components from SAP's Business Technology Platform to ensure performance and scalability requirements from the business and IT users at optimal TCO.

Optimization 

The business problem of deciding between the innumerable price reduction tactics can be solved via a mathematical optimization model. The choice of price reduction variables can be modelled by continuous, integer or binary variables. In addition there can be constraints on set of price points that are acceptable for example prices can only be $x.49 or $x.99. These influence whether the value variable can be continuous or restricted to integers.

Here is an example of set of such variables

 

 

 

 

Variables:​

   b : Binary variables if promotion should be chosen
   x : Continuous variables value if the promotion should be chosen
   y : Integer variables for how many units need to be bought by the consumer for the promotional incentive to be realized
  c : Binary variable for communication mechanism
  d : Binary variable for in-store display mechanism

 

 

 

 

The mathematical model then has constraints to ensure the variables behave in the desired way. Here is an a set of constraints to ensure the solution meets business needs.

 

 

 

 

Constraints:​

  Maximum number of offers
  Binding constraints between b and x and y​ # x and y can only take non-zero values if corresponding b variable is on i.e. 1 showing the promotion is chosen (and vice-versa)
  Maximum number of communication mechanisms
  Maximum number of display mechanism

 

 

 

Now we have modelled the variables and constraints that can model the business problem. However, if we use typical mathematical programming techniques like Linear or Mixed Integer Programming (LP or MIP) we have to describe the objective function as a linear term of the variables. The end user wants to find the variables which would give the maximum uplift or its derived targets. The uplift itself is known only via an ML model and is a function of the variables of the optimization model. We do not have an explicit linear model that can provide the uplift in terms of the variables. This is where Black-Box Optimization techniques come to the rescue, instead of defining the objective as a linear term we provide it to the model via User-defined functions.

User-defined Functions

We use the user-defined functionality provided by FICO Xpress.

The library provides functionality for expressing both the objective function and additional non-linear constraints via user-defined functions.

For our use-case the user defined function needs to provide the uplift or other derived target based on user selection as business users want to evaluate different strategies and tactics depending on the product, retailer, current market dynamics. Here is pseudo-code snippet showing the use of user-defined function

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

import xpress as xp
p = xp.problem("SAP BTP TPEO")

# Add Linear Variables and Constraints
#...

#Now add the user defined funtion variable and objective
target = xp.var(name = 'target')
p.addVariable(target)
p.addConstraint(target == xp.user(obj_func, b,x,y,c,d))
#...

def obj_func(b,x,y,c,d)
if target == 'POS'
   return uplift(b,x,y,c,d)
if target == 'GROSS_SALES'  #GES
   return cost_per_unit*uplift(b,x,y,c,d)
if target == 'NET_SALES'    #NES
   return net_sales(uplift(b,x,y,c,d))
if target == 'PROFITABILITY' #CP metrics
   net_sales = net_sales(uplift(b,x,y,c,d))
   investment_cost = investment_cost(b,x,y,c,d) #if cost is only a function of chosen promotion
   #or investment_cost = investment_cost(uplift(b,x,y,c,d)) #if cost is both a function of the uplift volume and chosen promotion
   return net_sales - cost
if target == 'ROI'
   return net_(sales - investment_cost)/investment_cost

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Optimal Solutions

The optimizer evaluates different options for promotional incentives and converges on the optimal solution. 

If the objective is purely uplift(incremental POS volume) or incremental Gross Sales(GES in the figure below) the optimizer chooses incentive which do not account for the additional investment required to achieve those targets.

An example optimizer solution development across the iterations and considering metrics like incremental uplift(POS), the optimal promotion is to give $3 off as discount.

However, now if you want to consider the investment needed and optimize for profitability the optimal solution might look for reduced discount that maximizes uplift as increasing discount increases the investment the manufacturer has to do by reducing its own margins and that of the retailer. The retailers would only agree to reduced price if they can still guarantee their minimum margins, which in term means the manufacturer has to give the retailer additional discount based on scanned units or bulk investments to ensure retailer still goes for the promotion.

For the above example now the optimal solution is to give only $0.50 off in discount, this reduces the uplift compared to before but ensures highest profit.

The optimizer can provide one single proposal which is optimized towards a particular target or a proposal weighted across multiple targets. It can also provide a selection of top performing proposals (promotional offers in this case) and allow the business user to select the best based on their current strategy. These proposal offers are used by the business users for benefit analysis.

Benefit Analysis

Ideally the account managers want to find options that are best across all targets but this is usually impossible as promotions with higher uplifts tend to be expensive and lower the ROI for the manufacturer and so the account managers find the best strategy for the product and retailer by analyzing the trade-offs of volumes vs profitability. The account manager needs to choose if they want to go for sales volume with first offer proposal(10 inthe figure) of Save $3 or drive profitability with second proposal of Price Reduction(12).

 

 

In some cases the solutions are easier for decision making for the account managers, for example for the proposals below the top 2 proposals with BOGO(Buy 1/Get 1 Free) and Price Reduction (with Feature Page) are competitive with BOGO being better for Gross Sales (GES) and Price Reduction is highest on Net Sales and Profitability metrics(CP)

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

With the optimized decision support system the account managers can now do the job they are really good. They can focus on the strategic decision making instead of trying out multiple options manually while still feeling they have not really explored the whole space of options out there.

Having a set of optimally selected proposals that account for the price elasticity seen from their historic data the account managers can do the next step of portfolio optimization (you guessed it, its yet another mathematical optimization problem, perhaps for another blog post). This helps them ensure overall profitability across all the products and retailers without exceeding their allocated budgets and providing their expertise and learnings from current market trends.

The account managers can take decisions grounded with learnings from historical promotions and optimized for their target while respecting the restrictions from their business partners(for example retailer margins), budget constraints, and consumer acceptable price points.

Architecture

To allow for dynamic scalability the following architecture was chosen for the above use-case. SAP's Business Technology Platform provides the UI, persistency on HANA Cloud,  and runtime via Kyma. As multiple business users need to run multiple scenarios for different products the system needs to dynamically scale up (and gracefully scale down after) during the promotional planning periods. This is enabled by scaling horizontally on Kyma as additional pods come up when the optimizer runs increase.

Conclusion

In many business areas the complete potential of data can only be unlocked by utilizing the learnings from predictive models to create prescriptive models which provide high-value decision support to the business users. This may seem as a challenging exercise at the outset but with the right set of ingredients this is achievable. The math and the technology are available to realize the benefits in a timely and cost efficient manner. This is achieved by empowering the experts with OptML decision support that combines Machine Learning and Mathematical Optimization together.

This blog post is based on recent customer work, for additional details feel free to contact the author.

entry.title: Easily create data snapshots from ABAP environment's CDS Views in SAP HANA Cloud, SAP HANA Database
entry.content:
Background

One of my colleagues introduce a new Smart Data Access (SDA) ABAP adapter wTaking Data Federation to the Next Level: Accessing Remote ABAP CDS View Entities in SAP HANA Cloud”. The adapter is available from QRC 01/2024 onwards. The blog explained the following steps in detail using SQL statements.

  • Creating a remote source to an ABAP system based on SAP ABAP Platform 2108 or later with CDS views bound to SQL Services.
  • Virtualizing a remote CDS view entities as virtual tables for federated queries.

To simplify the use of CDS views, the Data Replication application found in HANA Cloud Central is extended to support creating scheduled snapshots from such ABAP systems including S/4HANA. The Data Replication application in SAP HANA Cloud Central is the successor for Replication Service application available in SAP HANA Cockpit for SAP HANA Cloud which is no longer maintained.

The Data Replication now extends the source system to include S/4HANA on-prem, S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition by selecting SAP S/4HANA as source. Additionally, S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition or SAP BTP ABAP environment by selecting SAP S/4HANA Public Edition as source.

 

 

The two additional sources are nearly identical for setting the remote source except that SAP S/4HANA requires some additional information such as the cloud connector setup configurations and the client id. For SAP S/4HANA Cloud, it doesn’t require those additional information. The details of how to setup SQL Services and bind a CDS view entity can be referenced in the “Accessing ABAP-Managed Data Using SQL Services for Data Integration Scenarios” section.

How to configure the snapshot

Once the type of remote source is selected either as S/4HANA or S/4HANA Cloud, the connection properties needs to be entered to establish a connection to the remote source and get its metadata information.

 

 

 

The Service Path normally is fixed to “/sap/bc/sql/sql1/sap/s_privileged” but can be extended by ABAP team for future expansions.

Keep the default settings for Type Map = semantic and Uid Type = alias.

Setup Cloud Connector when trying to connect to S/4HANA on-prem or S/4HANA Private Edition that are behind firewalls.

As the connection is created successfully, Data Replication will get the list of CDS view entities. Select the entity to create a data snapshot in the SAP HANA Cloud environment as shown below.

After selecting the object, click confirm to start creating the snapshot(s). It will start loading all data of the CDS view to the replica table and the status will be shown as “Loading”.

As the data is all loaded, the status will change to “Completed” with Replication Schedule as “Manual Refresh”. The data can be manually refreshed again by clicking the circular arrow icon next to the Replication Schedule dropdown selection. Keep in mind that the exisiting data will truncated and the latest data loaded for each refresh.

If the snapshot(s) needs to be updated on a regular basis, select the object(s) and click “Set Replication Schedule” in the top right. The snapshot frequency can be setup as daily, weekly or monthly.

 

Conclusion

We have covered how to do the following in the Data Replication application available in SAP HANA Cloud Central

  • Create a data snapshot from CDS views 
  • Configure a schedule to periodically take snapshots

Combined with the snapshot data from CDS views and using the latest features available in SAP HANA Cloud for Intelligent Data Applications

The snapshot can use any CDS views that is binded to SQL Services. For real-time replication scenario, the Data Replication application plans to support it once it become available in the ABAP adapter layer. For SAP Datasphere scenarios, please refer to Creating replication flow for SQL Service exposure

entry.title: SAP HANA Cloud sessions at Devtoberfest 2024
entry.content:
Hello SAP HANA Enthusiasts!

Devtoberfest 2024 has concluded, and we hope you were able to join some of the sessions. If you missed any, no worries! Here is an overview of the SAP HANA Cloud sessions for your convenience.

🔵 Embed the latest from SAP HANA’s AI functions into your SAP BTP Application with @ChristophMorgen 

In this session, Christoph introduced newly added Machine Learning and AI functions designed to enhance your SAP BTP Intelligent Data Applications.

The features include data-parallel AutoML (such as segmented AutoML TimeSeries), functions for vector data analysis, multi-label classification and regression, and more from the latest SAP HANA Cloud releases, as well as the Predictive Analysis Library (PAL) and the Machine Learning client for SAP HANA.

🔵 SAP HANA Cloud Multi-Model with @mfath 

In this session, Markus focused on the Multi-Model capabilities of SAP HANA Cloud, with a particular emphasis on the vector and graph engines. Discover how a robust and scalable data management platform accelerates AI projects through rapid data processing, real-time analytics, and advanced algorithms.

🔵 Advanced Strategies for Intelligent Data Applications on SAP HANA Cloud with @VolkerSaggau , @shraddhashetty  and @breglerj 

This session explored advanced features of HDI and HANA Tooling. Learn from Jonathan, Volker, and Shraddha about optimizing deployment with HDI parameters, utilizing the Fabric Virtual Table in HDI, and leveraging advanced HANA tooling features, including AI-generated SQL and CDS.

Are you new to Devtoberfest?

Check more about the event at https://community.sap.com/t5/devtoberfest-blog-posts/devtoberfest-2024-welcome/ba-p/9358 by @thomas_jung 

Are you looking for sessions from previous editions?

Please check the Data && Analytics sessions at Devtoberfest 2023.

Enjoy your Devtoberfest 2024 experience!

 

--Vitaliy

entry.title: Speeding Up Analytics Reporting with the SAP HANA Cloud - analytics accelerator
entry.content:
Introduction to the Analytics Accelerator and MDS Cubes

SAP HANA Cloud enables complex analytical processing of immense data volumes on the fly. In some situations, however, the focus is not on online processing but on minimizing resource usage and optimizing performance. Wouldn’t it be great if in these situations an option existed that could be used to reduce resource consumption, while still being able to process larger data volumes and even offering improved performance once the real-time requirements can be lifted?

With the Analytics Accelerator, SAP offers an option to base SAP Analytics Cloud models on pre-processed and read-optimized data structures. These structures represent a sub model of a calculation view called MDS Cube.

An MDS Cube is stored on disk. Only those parts which are needed for query processing are pulled into main-memory. This reduces the memory footprint and cost.

MDS Cubes can have a tremendous effect on reducing resource consumption and speeding up queries in scenarios that involve complex calculations, large data volumes and a high number of concurrent users.

And the best thing is they are easy to create out of already existing calculation views. Watch the below demo to gain a first understanding of the impact MDS Cubes can have on your analytical scenario.
Curious? If you already have an SAC story running on a calculation view and want to leverage the strengths of an MDS Cube, please register for our Early Adopter Care program!

A long running scenario

Now, let’s have a closer look at the demo scenario. As of today we are using an SAC Story on top of a calculation view to analyze our Sales business for our products. We do have about 50 million facts.

Due to the complexity of the scenario and the multi-user requirements the SAP Analytics Cloud story takes significant time to open.

Lets’ see whether an MDS Cube can help us to speed up our Dashboard.

Creation of an MDS Cube

In SAP Business Application Studio, open the calculation view on which the story is based.

As you can see the calculation view defines a star join that combines facts with dimensional data. Nothing is special about this calculation view - yet.

The change comes now: If you open the tab MDS Cubes of the Semantics node, you can define the MDS Cube on which the SAC story should be based in the future. Let’s do it

We define a MDS Cube with name LIQUID_HORZON_MDS_CUBE

and make sure that all attributes and measures that will be required by the SAC story are included.

And that’s already it!

The lifecycle of the MDS Cube is tied to the lifecycle of the calculation view. This means if you deploy the calculation view the MDS Cube will be created. Analogously, the MDS Cube will be removed when you undeploy the calculation view. By this the MDS Cube adds no further complexity to your lifecycle management.

You can define multiple MDS Cubes for the same calculation view. Deployment of the calculation view builds the view and generates all MDS cubes that are defined for it, initially without data.

The Management of MDS Cubes

We've created our MDS Cube. The next step is to fill the MDS Cube with data.

To query metadata, load, or delete the data of MDS Cubes, a specific API exist. The API works with a built-in procedure that can be executed as SQL statement with different commands and parameters.

  • MANAGE_MDS_CUBE('<JSON MDS Cube API>',?)

Examples can be found here: API for Administrating MDS Cubes | SAP Help Portal

Let’s load data into the MDS Cube LIQUID_HORZON_MDS_CUBE.

To execute the procedure the database user needs

  • EXECUTE on SYS.MANAGE_MDS_CUBE
  • CREATE ANY on the schema in which the calculation view resides
  • SELECT on the calculation view

 

 

--load mds cube
CALL MANAGE_MDS_CUBE('
{
   "Cube": {
      "Command": "Reload",
      "Target": {
         "DataSource": {
            "SchemaName": "LIQUID_HORIZON",
            "ObjectName": "model.mdscube::LIQUID_HORIZON_LTD/mdscube/LIQUID_HORIZON_MDS_CUBE"
         }
      }
}',?);

 

 

After the data loading is finished, pre-aggregated data is stored in the MDS Cube in a highly optimized manner to speed up analytical queries. In contrast to a cache, the data is not stored as a flat data structure, but in a multidimensional, star-join-like structure that is optimized for tailored data access.

The Data in the MDS Cube reflects the state at the time that the MDS Cube was loaded.

Analytics with MDS Cubes

Let’s look at our SAC story but now based directly on the MDS Cube :

 

MDS Cubes can greatly speed up complex queries in scenarios with huge data volumes where multiple users run queries concurrently.

MDS Cubes therefore provide a powerful, simple and well integrated solution for heavy usage scenarios.

Summary

With MDS Cubes a powerful option is available to speed up analytic queries in SAC that are build on SAP HANA Cloud calculation views. MDS Cubes are easy to define based on existing calculation views; they are quickly loaded with data and straightforward to use within SAC stories.

If you have a use case where an SAC story is already based on a calculation view and you are interested in leveraging an MDS Cube, please register for our free of charge Early Adopter Care Program specifically tailored to MDS Cubes. The registration is open until February 28, 2025.

References

  • Speeding Up Analytics Reporting by Using MDS Cubes | SAP Help Portal
  • Define an MDS Cube Based on Elements in a Calculation View | SAP Help Portal
  • API for Administrating MDS Cubes | SAP Help Portal
  • Learning : Defining MDS Cubes

entry.title: SAP Developer News, October 31st, 2024
entry.content:
Podcast Version: https://podcast.opensap.info/sap-developers/2024/10/31/sap-developer-news-october-31st-2024/

DESCRIPTION

New CodeJam: Expose capabilities of SAP AI Core with the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model

  • Github repository: https://github.com/SAP-samples/codejam-cap-llm/tree/main
  • SAP CodeJam topics: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-codejam-blog-posts/sap-codejam-topics/ba-p/221407
  • Host a CodeJam: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-codejam-blog-posts/so-you-want-to-host-a-codejam-everything-you-need-to-know/ba-p/221415
  • SAP Cloud SDK for AI: https://github.com/SAP/ai-sdk-js/tree/main

SAP TechEd On Tour

  • SAP TechEd: https://www.sap.com/events/teched.html
  • ASUG Tech Connect 2024: https://events.asug.com/event/9a5f27ca-d742-45bb-85e7-a6597b952900/summary?RefId=sap-splash-page-home&utm_campaign=ATC_24&utm_medium=sap&utm_source=referral&utm_term=sap-splash-page-home
  • SAPinsider EMEA 2024: https://reg.eventmobi.com/emea-2024?utm_source=marketing&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=teched&utm_content=sapmarketing
  • SAUG-x 2024: https://www.saug.com.au/WhatsOn/SAUG-x2024
  • UKISUG Connect 2024: https://www.ukisugconnect.co.uk/techx-at-connect?utm_source=TechEd_Webpage&utm_medium=Website&utm_campaign=TECHx_at_Connect
  • DSAG-TechXchange 2025: https://dsag.de/neuigkeiten/sap-teched-goes-dsag-techxchange-english/

ML/AI in SAP Data Management products

  • Let’s Understand Knowledge Graph in 5 Simple Points: https://medium.com/@raja.gupta20/lets-understand-knowledge-graph-in-5-simple-points-36999c79df8e 
  • Connecting the Facts: SAP HANA Cloud’s Knowledge Graph Engine for Business Context: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/connecting-the-facts-sap-hana-cloud-s-knowledge-graph-engine-for-business/ba-p/13888597
  • Get Hands-On: Build an Intelligent Data Application powered by the SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/get-hands-on-build-an-intelligent-data-application-powered-by-the-sap-hana/ba-p/13916066
  • Accelerate the Budget Approval Process with SAP HANA Cloud vector engine: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-members/accelerate-the-budget-approval-process-with-sap-hana-cloud-vector-engine/ba-p/13884137
  • Turbocharging Machine Learning on SAP Data with Datasphere, FedML, and NVIDIA: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/turbocharging-machine-learning-on-sap-data-with-datasphere-fedml-and-nvidia/ba-p/13889054
  • SAP and DataRobot Partnership Updates 2024: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/sap-and-datarobot-partnership-updates-2024/ba-p/13916493

The Open Source Way podcast episode: R/3 on Linux

  • Blog post https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/release-of-new-podcast-episode-sap-r-3-port-to-linux/ba-p/13918445
  • Podcast episode https://podcast.opensap.info/open-source-way/2024/10/24/sap-r3-port-to-linux/

CHAPTER TITLES

0:00 Intro

0:10 New CodeJam: Expose capabilities of SAP AI Core with the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model

0:55 TechEd On Tour

1:58 ML/AI in SAP Data Management products

3:58 The Open Source Way podcast episode: R/3 on Linux

Transcription

[Intro] This is the SAP Developer News for October 31st, 2024.

[Kevin] If you followed the announcements from SAP TechEd 2024, you know that there is a brand new SAP Cloud SDK for AI for JavaScript, Java, and ABAP. So I thought why not create a brand new code gem to teach you how to utilize AI capabilities within your CAP application using the SAP Cloud SDK for AI JavaScript version. So that code gem is out right now. So go ahead, visit the community.sap.com to see if there is a CodeJam happening right nearby you, or if you want to host one yourself. There is a ton of description on how to do that. And hopefully I'll see you next year in a CodeJam talking about AI and CAP. I can't wait.

[Promo] It's a long way from Philly to Heidelberg, but the show must go on. We're late, aren't we? Yeah, we're late. We all know there's nothing like the real thing. Luckily, we've got our best people on the job. Oh, sorry, Josh. Josh, if you pulled your own weight, we wouldn't have to carry you through this place.

[Witalij] We at SAP are enhancing our cloud native data management products with advanced machine learning and artificial intelligence technologies, providing robust tools for developers and data scientists. First, the SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph Engine announced at SAP TechEd Virtual is essential for developers and data scientists among you working with large language models or LLMs generator AI, as it facilitates the extraction of complex insights from bus data sets. Check Shabana's post to find out more.

Next, with the release of SAP HANA Cloud's Vector Engine, our development team built an insightful Movie Inside demo app to showcase the vector-based querying and storage capabilities. This demo illustrates the Vector Engine's role in enhancing LLM responses with contextual data. The entire demo is now available to you, providing a hands-on experience for developers and tech enthusiasts like you to explore the vector engine's capabilities and applications. To see such a practical implementation, check one of the partner applications known as SBF or Smart Budgeting and Forecasting.

Summarizing long documents and querying using the trivial augmented generation with SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine, this application enables a quicker understanding of the attachments to budget request forms.

In addition, SAP DataSphere, in collaboration with NVIDIA, integrates Federated Machine Learning, or FEDML. This setup allows data scientists to conduct extensive ML experiments on SAP business data while ensuring data security and stellar performance.

Finally, the integration of data robots' AI models into SAP environments empowers developers and data scientists to embed AI-driven insights directly into their workflow. The partnership enhances the technical capabilities of SAP data management tools, making it easier to deploy and manage AI models within existing systems.

[DJ] Did you know that SAP, in particular the open source program office within SAP has a podcast show? It's been running now four years and to celebrate their fourth anniversary, they've published a monumentally epic episode. What is the episode about? Well, in my opinion, it's about one of the few major, major milestones in SAP software history, and that is the port of SAP R/3 to Linux. Now, you might think, oh, it's a big deal, but back then, and this was in the early 1990s, back then it was a massive deal. I was super lucky to be around at the time to get to know the two main actors in this affair, in this amazing event, Christoph Rohland and Harald Kuck. And these two, Christoph and Harald, are guests on this particular anniversary episode of the podcast show to talk about how it came to be, what happened. It's an amazing story. I can't recommend this episode strongly enough. Anyway, get the podcast show into your favourite podcast player, have a listen, and while you're at it, also have a look at this blog post from Ulrika, which talks a little bit more about the show, and also specifically about the milestone that Harold and Christoph will relate to you. So, see you online, and by the way, Happy birthday, the open-source way.

entry.title: Step by Step guide to creating remote data in HDI
entry.content:
These are the steps that need to be proceeded to get a virtual table, a snapshot table or a replica table(RTR) in an HDI container:

  1. Preparation to work with remote resources
  2. Remote source definition
  3. Role in SAP HANA Cloud to allow remote definitions,
  4. User in SAP HANA Cloud for granting the rights,
  5. Custom User Provides Service (CUPS) to grant the rights to HDI containers
  6. HDI Grants file for CUPSHDI Virtual Table definition
  7. HDI Fabric Virtual Table

Depending on your responsibility on your SAP HANA Cloud you may only be in allowed to do certain of these steps or these steps have already been proceeded on your instance of SAP HANA Cloud.

1. Preparation to work with remote resources

Working with DBADMIN role:

Views we will use:

All starts with a certificate. In order to have a secure connection to another SAP HANA Cloud or SAP HANA Platform a certificate is needed. Latest certificate for "remote source" = another SAP HANA or database is the so called "DigiCert TLS RSA4096 Root G5". See more HERE

Attention: Check if you need to create a certificate at all. SAP now provides a preset of certificates (see below)

Retrieve a certificate:

curl -O https://cacerts.digicert.com/DigiCertTLSRSA4096RootG5.crt.pem

You can import this pem file in the terminal console of SAP BUILD / SAP Business Application Studio.

Now you have to import the certificate in SAP HANA Cloud:

-- create certificate
CREATE CERTIFICATE FROM
'-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE ... -----END CERTIFICATE-----'
-- replace this string with your pem string!
COMMENT 'HC';

In my case SAP provided the certificate already, so the create statement will fail for a good reason:

In general certificates will expire sooner or later. In our case, more on the later side. Still, one should keep attention to this. Your remote connection will fail, once an expired certificate is passed. You can set alarms to watch this in "Alert Notification System ANS" or "Cloud ALM".

Keep the CERTIFICATE_ID. It is needed for the upcoming step. Next, you need a PSE (personal security environment):

 

It is important to define the PURPOSE of the PSE. It has to be "REMOTE SOURCE".

SET PSE REMOTESOURCE PURPOSE REMOTE SOURCE;

As you can see, one can also assign several certificates to a PSE.

This closes the discussion for certificates and the preparation.

2. Remote source definition

Now that we have a PSE with PURPOSE "REMOTE SOURCE" communication with another SAP HANA should be possible. SAP HANA will use this information while connecting to others.

CREATE REMOTE SOURCE "HC_REMOTESOURCE"
ADAPTER "HANAODBC"
CONFIGURATION 
'<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<ConnectionProperties name="connectionproperties">
   <PropertyEntry name="adapterversion">1.0</PropertyEntry>
   <PropertyEntry name="connectionmode">Adapter Properties</PropertyEntry>
   <PropertyEntry name="dsn"></PropertyEntry>
   <PropertyEntry name="driver">libodbcHDB.so</PropertyEntry>
   <PropertyEntry name="server"> "Servername" </PropertyEntry>
   <PropertyEntry name="port">443</PropertyEntry>
   <PropertyEntry name="extraadapterproperties">encrypt=TRUE</PropertyEntry>
</ConnectionProperties>'
WITH CREDENTIAL TYPE 'PASSWORD'
USING 'user=<user_name>;password=<password>';

As an alternative, it is recommend to use the GUI in the SAP HANA Database Explorer - at least at the first time.

Keep it simple and USE EXISTING CREDENTIALS as well as a "Technical User".

Now, if you DON'T specify "Use existing credentials" you can add a specific PSE to your connection. Otherwise, the first one is the general purpose one.

With "Technical User" you can use a USER from the remote source to get access to the data you want and he is allowed.

Hint: Take a simple remote table and build your first "Virtual Table" on SCHEMA-level. This is just to prove that the communication is working.

I use the "VT_<REMOTETABLENAME>" prefix to immediately see my VT(Virtual Tables). Something like VT_PERSON for example. Keep in mind that there is only ONE replica of a REMOTETABLE possible in the target SAP HANA. So do not use your desired tables for this test or DROP that VT_TABLE after the successful test.

3. Role in SAP HANA Cloud for allow remote definitions

Now in HDI the users have very limited rights. One of the limitations is the use of remote resources.

System privileges are needed to work properly.

  • CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE
  • CREATE REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION
Now someone in the database has to provide these privileges to the HDI container.
Rather than to have a dedicated user, my recommendation is to use a ROLE.
Also my recommendation is to use a ROLE GROUP such as "HDI_GRANTER".
So we have a
ROLE GROUP        HDI_GRANTOR_GROUP
ROLE               HC_REMOTESOURCE_GRANTOR
 
In the next steps I use SAP HANA Cloud Central to do the needed steps. Please note, they can be done in SQL as well.
 
 
 
Below is a user with the respective privileges (DBADMIN would be one, but in production it should be more detailed)
 
CREATE ROLEGROUP HDI_GRANTOR_GROUP;
--DROP ROLE HC_REMOTESOURCE_GRANTOR;

CREATE ROLE HC_REMOTESOURCE_GRANTOR SET ROLEGROUP HDI_GRANTOR_GROUP;
GRANT CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE, CREATE REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION 
ON REMOTE SOURCE HC_REMOTESOURCE TO HC_REMOTESOURCE_GRANTOR WITH GRANT OPTION;
 
 
Now we have role that is able to GRANT others the privilege to create virtual tables and remote subscriptions, but ONLY on the HC_REMOTESOURCE (HANA Cloud remote source) where the original table lives.
If you want to control further the capabilities of this ROLE use the REMOTESOURCE technical user to limit the access.
Next, we need a user that can be assigned to this role. Yes, we could have this quick and easy directly on the user, but this approach allows to be much more modular.

4. User in SAP HANA Cloud for granting the rights

Next step is the same with the user that should get this role. 

USER GROUP    GRANTOR_GROUP

    USER              HC_REMOTESOURCE_GRANTER_USER

The only task of this user is: GRANT the privileges on this REMOTESOURCE to the HDI container. Now, we also need the role be assigned to this user:

CREATE USER DEMO_GRANTOR PASSWORD Password1 
  NO FORCE_FIRST_PASSWORD_CHANGE 
  VALID UNTIL FOREVER
SET USERGROUP DEFAULT;
GRANT HC_REMOTESOURCE_GRANTOR TO DEMO_GRANTOR WITH ADMIN OPTION;

Now there is HC_REMOTESOURCE_GRANTOR that is able to GRANT the privileges we need into the HDI Container.

A bit over the top, but now you have a structure to put in more roles on Roles and Users on HDI privileges and right.

Why did we do all this: Someone has to give our HDI container the rights to use REMOTESOURCE and even to create subscriptions. So when the container gets deployed your should grant the right to 2 users:

1. the Container owner

2. the RunTime user

The MTA deployment must know where to find the user that can grant all the priviliges to the HDI-container.

So next we need a so called USER PROVIDES SERVICE in our CloudFoundry space.

5. Custom User Provides Service (CUPS) to grant the rights to HDI containers

Next step is to create a CUPS (Custom User Provided Service).

There is a wizard in BAS that helps you to get this done easily:

Add a CUPS to your project:

Define properties:

1.) Add a new "Database Connection"

2.) Select "Create user-provides service instance"

You have to be connected to CloudFoundry.

This will add a CUPS into your dev space

and results in a (Custom) User Provided Service (CUPS). You will see the user provides services in your SPACE. 

  • HDI Grants file for CUPS

Next is to define the GRANT-file that you you just generated.

With the next deployment this file (1) will be granted to OO-User (2) and RT-User (3) with the privileges (4)

or in text mode:

 

 

 

{
   "ServiceName_2": {
      "application_user": {
         "global_object_privileges": [
            {
               "type": "REMOTE SOURCE",
               "name": "HC_REMOTESOURCE",
               "privileges": [
                  "CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE",
                  "CREATE REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION"
               ]
            }
         ]
      },
      "object_owner": {
         "global_object_privileges": [
            {
               "type": "REMOTE SOURCE",
               "name": "HC_REMOTESOURCE",
               "privileges": [
                  "CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE",
                  "CREATE REMOTE SUBSCRIPTION"
               ]
            }
         ]
      }
   }
}

 

 

 

With the next deployment these rights also become effective in the BAS environment. You should do that to ease the next step.

  • HDI Virtual Table Definition

I have a second SAP HANA Cloud Instance with a user. 

Now that the rights are there to use a remote resources as our "HC_REMOTERESOURCE", we can use the HDI artifact <object_name>.hdbvirtualtable.

For example, create a file call FVT.hdbvirtualtable.

The graphical editor should look like this:

On the source SAP HANA Cloud I prepared a user called "MYAPPLCIATION" which I also used as the Technical User in my remote connection. In the source schema "MYAPPLICATION" you have to create the table you want to use.

Sample:

CREATE COLUMN TABLE PERSON (
 ID INTEGER, 
 FIRST_NAME VARCHAR(100), 
 LAST_NAME VARCHAR(100)
 );

Now, insert some values,

INSERT INTO PERSON values(1, 'Albert','Einstein');
INSERT INTO PERSON values(2, 'Robert','Oppenheimer');

Give your virtual table a name. My personal preference is VT_<orginalTableName>. So I can spot remote tables immediately.

Please delete the file that is often created as well FVT.hdbvirtualtableconfig. You do not need this here. It is a topic for another blog.

We are nearly at the end. Now you can deploy again and you will have the VT_PERSON on your side.

You should now see 2 rows in the VT_PERSON table. This is table will retrieve the data from the source SAP HANA.

  • HDI Fabric Virtual Table

Now with FVT we can make this table either a snapshot or synchronized replica of the original table.

The decision is driven by performance, cost and volatility of the source table and the need to reflect that in the target table.

From HDI side we have one object that will help to get either of both:

<objectname>.hdbfabricvirtualtable

It may be that your .hdiconfig is not fully configured. Please check if this entry in present:

If you do not find this plugin-name, please add it the the config file. 

"hdbfabricvirtualtable" : { 
   "plugin_name" : "com.sap.hana.di.sharedreplica"
 },

Now create file like FVT.hdifabricvirtual table:

with this content:

The full syntax would be:

SHARED [SNAPSHOT] REPLICA [PAGE|COLUMN LOADABLE] ON <vt_name>

and the following run time operations can be done with the FVT:

 

Now this is very large blog with a lot of input. Once you have established remote connections, granting tasks gets quite simple. I put all of this in one blog because there are many places where you have to search for the individual topics.

Have fun with federation and replication.

 

References:

A very good introduction as learning journey:

https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/hana-cloud-mission-extend-05.html

Chapter 1

  • 3399573 - HANA Cloud Switch of DigiCert Root Certificate
  • 3327214 - Root Certificate Replacement in the SAP BTP, Cloud Foundry Environment
  • Import Certificates for SSL Connections to Remote Sources

entry.title: SAP CodeJam HANA ML in Wrocław 2024-10 Recap
entry.content:
At the end of October, we had the Getting Started with Machine Learning using SAP HANA and Python in Wrocław, Poland, kindly hosted by LeverX and organized by @Olena_Nadtochii. 

We had a fantastic group of participants, ranging from seasoned SAP veterans...

...to fresh talents who recently graduated from university.

This SAP CodeJam took place in a historical building—the former seat of the Faculty of Pharmacy of Wrocław University: a part of the university buildings' frontage, stretching along the Odra River in the northern part of the Old Town. The view from the top was breathtaking: from the Gothic Cathedral on the right to Concordia Design on the left—the Building of the Year 2021, according to ArchDaily.

And the food was equally fabulous.

I’d like to thank all the participants who joined me for this final SAP CodeJam of 2024!

The 2024 tour included 14 stops for me, and now it has come to an end.

Now, the next stops are in 2025, and I hope to see you in Spain ( @ajmaradiaga ), UAE ( @athavanraja ), India ( @Shilpa_Shankar ). Would you like to host such an SAP CodeJam in your location? Here are the details: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/quot-getting-started-with-machine-learning-usin...

entry.title: How to handle UUID in APL
entry.content:
As the adoption of UUIDs (Universally Unique Identifier) and GUIDs (Globally Unique Identifier) becomes prevalent, APL (Automated Predictive Library) users may face the case where their training dataset contains such IDs. These unique identifiers act as a primary key to differentiate each row in your invoices table for example, or your customers table. This blog shows how APL can work with input data containing a UUID. We will cover the two phases of machine learning: Train and Predict.

The UUID in SAP HANA

If you run the following SQL in SAP HANA:

 

 

select SYSUUID as "MY_UUID" from dummy

 

 

you obtain a big alpha-numeric value:

 

 

Now, create a view from it just to check the datatype:

 

 

create view MY_APL_VIEW as
select SYSUUID as "MY_UUID" from dummy;

select view_name, column_name, data_type_name
from view_columns where view_name = 'MY_APL_VIEW';

 

 

 

 

The UUID is stored as a VARBINARY, but this datatype is not supported in APL, therefore we will convert the UUID into a hexadecimal string. This conversion can be done with the function: bintohex().

 

Training dataset with a UUID

We need to train a classification model against this dataset:

 

 

 

 

 

Because the table contains a UUID, we must convert that column using the script below:

 

 

select 
 BINTOHEX("UUID") as "Id",
 "Days_to_Report",
 "Bodily_Injury", 
 "Property_Damage",
 "Age",
 "Gender",
 "Income",
 "Fraud" 
from 
 "APL_SAMPLES"."ACCIDENT_CLAIMS"

 

 

Make the conversion either via a SQL view if you are working with APL SQL, or via a dataframe if you are working with APL Python. An example of dataframe based on a select statement is shown here:

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/multiclass-classification-with-apl-automated-predictive-library/ba-p/13545072

The SQL view for training the APL model looks like this:

 

 

 

 

 

Inference dataset with a UUID

The inference dataset requires the same conversion before you can run the APL predictions:

 

 

select 
 BINTOHEX("UUID") as "Id",
 "Days_to_Report",
 "Bodily_Injury", 
 "Property_Damage",
 "Age",
 "Gender",
 "Income"
from 
 "APL_SAMPLES"."ACCIDENT_NEW_CLAIMS"

 

 

The APL output from predicting claims fraud contains Id values that are in hexadecimal:

 

 

 

We must convert back the Id so that the APL output table can be joined to the input table:

 

 

select N.*, "gb_decision_Fraud" as "Prediction" 
from APPLY_OUT O, APL_SAMPLES.ACCIDENT_NEW_CLAIMS N
where HEXTOBIN(O."Id") = N."UUID"

 

 

 

To know more about APL

entry.title: Calling All SAP Partners: Join the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Cycle-3 2024!
entry.content:
Attention SAP partners! We're excited to let you know that the third cycle 2024 of the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative is in full swing, offering you an exclusive opportunity to collaborate with SAP on shaping the future of our products. By working closely with our developers and product managers, you'll gain early access to innovative development ideas and play a direct role in influencing SAP’s product strategy. 

This is your chance to make an impact and contribute your insights to upcoming SAP products and applications. Don't miss out - register for current projects by November 15, 2024! These projects are specifically open to SAP partners, and we look forward to hearing your valuable perspectives and ideas:

SAP S/4HANA & SAP S/4HANA Cloud

  • Composable Enterprise: Reimagining Enterprise Software for Unprecedented Business Agility 
  • Help Where and When You Need It: User Assistance in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • Improve your Post Upgrade Tests(PUT) Experience - An Integrated and Smart approach to PUT
  • Insightful Analytics for Actual Costing
  • Joule applications to SAP S/4HANA Utilities
  • Monitoring End to End Master Data Replications in SAP Cloud ALM
  • New Cost Settlement for real estate scenarios (Service Charge Settlement / CAM)
  • Purpose as base for processing of S/4HANA Business Partner personal data – concept and migration”
  • Research: SAP S/4HANA migration cockpit - AI: Field Mapping Proposal

SAP Business Technology Platform

  • AI Agent Builder - A Low-Code Innovation with SAP Build
  • AI-Powered Autonomous Sandbox
  • Discover SAP Build Process Automation Triggers: Empowering Experts to Automate and Extend Use Cases
  • GenAI Content Provenance and Authenticity via Watermarking
  • Knowledge Graph RAG on unstructured Data

Customer Experience

  • Redesigned SAP Sales Cloud, add-in for Microsoft Outlook

Data & Analytics

  • SAP HANA Database Migration to SAP HANA Cloud (XSC,XSA,CAP)

SAP Globalization

  • AI-supported Extension of Electronic Document Processing of SAP Document and Reporting Compliance
  • Localization as a Self-Service for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition with Business AI

Lifecycle Management

  • Business Service Management in SAP Cloud ALM operations
  • Clean Core / RISE with SAP Methodology Dashboard in SAP Cloud ALM

Others

  • Early Talent Management with SAP SuccessFactors

We believe that collaboration is key to unlocking innovation. By joining forces with our customers and partners, we can collectively shape the future of SAP’s products and change the way businesses operate.

Don't delay – register today on influence.sap.com and be a part of the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Cycle 3 in 2024!

Warm regards,

Your SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Team

Follow-up after registration: When you register for a project, you will be invited to an introductory call with the SAP project lead. At this point further participation is optional. Typically, all activities are governed by the Feedback Agreement with SAP.

entry.title: SAP Integration Suiteを使ってSAP Datasphereからデータを抽出する方法 JDBC編
entry.content:
はじめに

SAP の連携ツールである SAP Integration Suite を使用して SAP Datasphere からデータを抽出するには、以下の2つの方法があります。本ブログでは②の方法について紹介します。

① SAP Datasphere の OData サービスを利用したデータ抽出(前回のブログにて記載)

② SAP Datasphere データベースユーザでの JDBCを利用したデータ抽出(本ブログ)

今回は下記のバージョンで検証を行いました。
SAP Datasphere : バージョン2024.16.46
SAP Integration Suite : バージョン 3.66.0

手順

  1. SAP Datasphere でデータベースユーザを作成する
  2. SAP Integration Suite で JDBC データソースを作成する
  3. SAP Integration Suite で Integration Flow を作成する
  4. SAP Integration Suite で Integration Flow をデプロイする
  5. SAP Integration Suite で Integration Flow の動作を確認する

ポイント

  • SAP Integration Suite 側で SAP HANA Cloud の JDBC ドライバはデフォルトでインストールされているため、ユーザ側で JDBC ドライバをアップロードする必要はありません
  • SAP Integration Suite から SAP Datasphere への接続はデータベースユーザでおこなうため、データベースユーザが参照できる範囲のオブジェクトのデータが抽出できます

ここから具体的な方法を紹介します。

手順1. SAP Datasphere で データベースユーザを作成する

※すでに作成済みの場合は飛ばしてもかまいません

1.SAP Datasphere にログインします

2.スペース管理から対象となるスペースを選択し、[編集] ボタンをクリックします

3.データベースアクセスタブに移動します

4.データベースユーザの [作成] ボタンをクリックします

 

5.データベースユーザの作成ダイアログで以下を入力し、[作成] ボタンをクリックします

  • データベースユーザ名の接尾辞: 任意のユーザ名（この例では URESHINO）
  • スペーススキーマの読み込みアクセスを有効化 (SQL): チェックする
  • その他の設定: （任意）

6.[保存]　ボタンをクリックして設定を保存し、[デプロイ] ボタンをクリックしてデプロイします

7.ステータスが”有効”になったことを確認し、[情報ダイアログを開く] ボタン(iのマーク)をクリックします

8.データベースユーザ詳細画面で [新しいパスワードを申請] ボタンをクリックして、データベースユーザのパスワードを発行します

9.発行されたパスワードをメモ帳などにコピーしておきます
※ JDBC 接続を作成する際に、データベースユーザ名とホスト名、パスワードが必要になります

手順2. Integration Suite でのJDBCデータソースの作成

SAP Integration Suite から SAP Datasphere に JDBC 接続するための JDBC データソースを作成します

1.SAP Integration Suite にログインします

2.Monitor > Integrations and APIs をクリックします

3.JDBC Material のタイルをクリックします

4.JDBC Data Source タブを開き、[Add] ボタンをクリックします

5.Add JDBC Data Source ダイアログで以下の情報を入力し、[Deploy] ボタンをクリックします

  • Name: 任意の JDBC データソース名（この例では Datasphere_JP）
  • Description: 任意
  • Database Type: SAP HANA Cloud
  • User: SAP Datasphere のデータベースユーザ
  • Password: SAP Datasphere データベースユーザのパスワード
  • JDBC URL: jdbc:sap://{手順1-9 SAP Datasphere ホスト名}:443/?encrypt=true

 

6.JDBC Data Source の一覧画面で Status が Deployed になっていることを確認します

手順3. Integration SuiteでIntegration Flowの作成

1.SAP Integration Suite のホームページから Design > Integrations and APIs をクリックします

2.[Create] ボタンをクリックしてコンテンツパッケージを作成します

3.以下の情報を入力して [Save] ボタンをクリックします

  • Name: 任意のパッケージ名（この例では Datasphere_JDBC_Integration_Flow）
  • Technical Name: Name の内容から自動設定
  • Short Description: 任意の概要説明

4.Artifacts タブに移動します

5.Add をクリックして表示されるコンテキストメニューから [Integration Flow] を選択します

 

6.Add Integration Flow ダイアログで以下の情報を入力し、[Add] ボタンをクリックします

  • Create を選択
  • Name: 任意の Integration Flow 名（この例では Datasphere_JDBC_Integration_Flow）
  • ID: Name から自動入力
  • Runtime Profile: Cloud Integration
  • Description: 任意の説明
  • Sender: 空白
  • Receiver: 空白

​デプロイ時に一回だけ起動し、SAP Datasphere からデータを取得する Integration Flow を作成します

7.Artifacts から追加した Integration Flow を選択します

8.右のような Integration Flow を作成します

  • Request Reply から Receiver への接続では、[JDBC] を選択します

 

9.Content Modifier の Message Body タブをクリックし、以下の情報を入力します

  • Type: Expression
  • Body: 実行する SQL 文（この例では SELECT * FROM “{スペース名}”.”V_CutomerOrders”）

10.JDBC の Connection タブをクリックし、以下の情報を入力します

  • JDBC Data Source Alias: JDBC データソースの作成の手順で作成した JDBC データソース名（この例では Datasphere_JDBC）

手順4. Integration SuiteでIntegration Flowのデプロイ

作成した Integration Flow をデプロイします

1.[Save] ボタンをクリックします 

2.[Deploy] ボタンをクリックします

3.Confirmation ダイアログで Runtime Profile を選択して、[Yes] ボタンをクリックします

4.Deployment ダイアログで [OK] ボタンをクリックします

5.Deployment Status タブをクリックし、Deployment Status と Runtime Status を確認します

6.Navigate to Manage Integration Content リンクをクリックします

7.Manage Integration Content 画面で実行状態を確認します

8.Integration Flow の実行をトレースするためにLog Configuration の Log Level を Trace に変更します

9.Integration Flow の Edit 画面で再度 [Deploy] ボタンをクリックし、Integration Flow をデプロイします

手順5. Integration SuiteでIntegration Flowの実行結果の確認

1.Monitor > Integrations and APIs をクリックします

2.Monitor Message Processing の All Artifacts タイルをクリックします

3.作成・デプロイした Integration Flow を選択します

4.Logs タブをクリックし、Log Level の Trace リンクをクリックします

5.Run Steps の End をクリックします

6.Message Content をクリックします

7.Payload タブをクリックして、データが取得できていることを確認します（XML形式）

以上ですべての手順が完了しました。最後までお読みいただきありがとうございました。

Special thanks to Ikeguchi Daisuke-san!

entry.title: How to create a live connection between SAP Analytics Cloud and AWS Redshift
entry.content:
If you’re looking to set up an Acquired Data connection between SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) and AWS Redshift, you can create an SQL connection directly from SAC using SAP Cloud Connector and SAP Cloud Agent. Check out my previous blog post for a step-by-step guide: How to create an imported connection between SAP Analytics Cloud and AWS Redshift

But if you need a Live Data connection, you will need a third-party software, or set up the SAP Data Provisioning Agent (DP Agent) and create this Live Data connection through an SAP HANA Cloud database instance on the SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP). In this blog post, I’ll walk you through this process.

With this approach, we are adding an intermediate layer (SAP HANA Cloud) that not only enables the Live Data connection between SAC and Redshift, with an amazing performance and at an acceptable cost, but also brings additional benefits to SAC that are not possible with other approaches, including:

  • Integrate multiple sources: integrate data in SAC not only from Redshift, but also from other source systems: on-premise, cloud, SAP, non-SAP, via Virtual access or Replication.
  • User Authorizations: implement user authorizations in SAC using SAP HANA’s authorization features, such as user groups, roles, and privileges (both static and dynamic).
  • Connection Security: implement secure connections using multiple protocols (SAML, JWT, OpenID), data anonymization, encryption, access controls, certifications, and other built-in security features.
  • Data Modeling: build data models in SAP HANA, enabling SAC functionalities like parameters, hierarchies, data blending, support for diverse data types, and more, that are not possible with other approaches.

 

Steps to follow

First, we will need to install and set up DP Agent on a dedicated server. After that, we will connect HANA Cloud to Redshift. Then, we will connect the Plain Schema with the HDI Container in HANA Cloud. Finally, we will create a Live Data connection from SAC to HANA Cloud.

 

Integration architecture

 

Step 1 - Connect Redshift with HANA Cloud

1.1 - DP Agent - Installation and configuration

  • Download the DP Agent – follow this link.
  • Install the DP Agent on Windows – follow this link.
  • Configure the DP Agent – follow this link.

1.2 - HANA DBX – Connect to AWS Redshift

  • Create a remote source using HANA Database Explorer – follow this link.

 

Step 2 - Connect Plain Schema with HDI Container in HANA Cloud

2.1 - HANA DBX – Create the schema and virtual objects

  • Create the schema where the virtual objects will be created, create the virtual objects in that schema, create the Application user, create the Access role for that schema, and assign the role to the Application user. This tutorial by Thomas Jung is outstanding.

2.2 - BTP Cockpit – Activate and configure BAS

  • Configure the BAS entitlement, subscribe to BAS, and grant user permissions – follow this link.

2.3 - BAS – Create a Dev Space and a HANA database project

  • Configure BAS following this tutorial by Thomas Jung.

2.4 - BTP Cockpit – Create a User-Provided HDI instance

  • You can create it from BAS, by adding a Database connection. The hdbgrants file is automatically created – follow steps 1 and 2 of this tutorial.

2.5 - BAS - Connect HDI container with schema

  • Create hdbsynonyms file and create the synonyms to connect the HDI container with the schema–  follow steps 3 and 4 of this tutorial.

2.6 - BAS – Create a calculation view

  • Create a calculation view, and add the tables to the nodes using the previous synonyms – Follow this tutorial.

 

Step 3 - Connect HANA Cloud with SAC

3.1 - SAC – Create a live connection to HANA Cloud

  • Create a live data connection to SAP HANA Cloud using an "SAP HANA Cloud" connection. For single sign-on (SSO), you will need to enable a custom SAML identity provider - follow this link.

3.2 - SAC – Create a model

  • Create a model using the live data connection – follow this link.

3.3 - SAC - Create a story / insight

  • Create a new story (optimized) using the model – follow this link.
  • You can also create an insight following this link.

 

Now you are ready to create stories in SAC using a live data connection from Redshift!

If you run into any issues along the way, feel free to leave a comment—I’d be happy to assist.



Kind regards,

Carlos Pinto

Saclab Consulting

entry.title: Guided Wizards for JWT/SAML identity providers in HANA Cloud Central and SAP HANA Cockpit: Part 1
entry.content:
As you may know, within HANA Tooling (either it HANA Cloud Central* or HANA Cockpit) you have an opportunity to set up your own identity providers to make Single-Sign-On possible.  

From now on, to simplify this journey and give you a smooth and approachable way of setting them up, we are introducing Guided Provider Creation of those identity providers for JWT and SAML. 

The guided app wizard streamlines identity provider integration by automatically reading configurations, certificates, and keys from OpenID providers, reducing manual effort and errors. It combines the functions of the JWT provider, Certificates, and Certificate Collections apps, simplifying identity management and enhancing security in one unified solution. This consolidation minimizes operational complexity and costs, allowing businesses to efficiently manage identity configurations at scale. 

For more details on Identity Providers, please see: 

  • „Configuration Workflow for Authentication” section of SAP HANA Cloud Security documentation. 
  • „Managing Identity Providers for User Authentication” section of SAP HANA Administration with SAP HANA Cockpit documentation.

Illustration of the wizard usage 

In this guide, we will use the “JWT identity provider” setup as an example for our demo. In principle, the SAML provider setup follows the same logic. 

As usual, this guided provider app you can find within “User and Authorization Management” app in the section “Identity providers” (see screenshot below).

Step 0. To make sure you have access to this application, the following system privileges are required when you use SAP HANA Cloud Central: 

  • CREATE JWT PROVIDER 
  • CERTIFICATE ADMIN 
  • TRUST ADMIN 

If you use SAP HANA Database 2 on-premise and SAP HANA Cockpit accordingly, the following privileges are required: 

  • USER ADMIN 
  • CERTIFICATE ADMIN 
  • TRUST ADMIN 

On Step 1, the required system privileges are checked. If privileges are missing, an error message shows which ones are missing. 

Step 1. If the user has all the required privileges mentioned before, you need to provide a “JWT Identity Provider Name.” 

While you type the name in, it is checked for validity and whether there is already a JWT identity provider with the given name. You cannot proceed to the next step if the name is invalid or duplicate — you will be notified about that. 

Step 2. Provide the URL to the JWT OpenID Configuration of your OpenID provider 

This is the URL where the OpenID configuration of your JWT provider will be read from, including available claims and certificates or public keys. As the wizard mentions, usually, this is the issuer URL followed by the ‘/.well-known/openid-configuration’ path. The full URL your input provides (with mandatory scheme and URL ending) is displayed for you to check. 

When pressing the button for the next step, the configuration is read and evaluated. If the configuration cannot be read with the provided link, or if required information is missing in the read result, an error shows. 

The progress is reset to the "JWT OpenID Configuration" step. 

If the configuration can be read successfully, the wizard progresses to Step 3.

Step 3. Additional settings  

Some additional settings for the JWT identity provider must be supplied like “Priority” and “JWT identity Claim”: 

  • Priority: used to determine the order in which the identity providers with the same issuer URL are checked when an incoming token is mapped to an identity provider in the instance. 
  • The priority value is between 1 (lowest) and 255 (highest). By default, the value is set to 100. 
  • The identity provider with the highest priority set (255) is checked first. 
  • If there is already JWT identity provider defined for the Issuer URL, the new provider has to have a different priority. 
  • JWT Identity Claim: contains the user's unique identifier, such as username or email. It is used for user authentication and mapping. It can vary depending on the provider. 
  • It can be selected from the list of supported claims as they are defined in the OpenID configuration 

Step 4. You can also provide “Additional claims” 

The claims in the section "Other" list must all be unique and for each line with a selected claim, a value must be entered, otherwise you will not be able to proceed. You do not have to worry about the empty lines – they will be simply ignored. With that, we can go the step of setting up certification collection. 

Step 5. Here, a new certificate collection will be created for the JWT provider 

The creation of a certificate collection is needed for verifying the signature of the JWT provider tokens. 

The certificates and/or public keys for this collection will be taken from the OpenID configuration, imported into the SAP HANA Cloud and added to the new certificate collection by the wizard application. 

A standard name for the collection will be proposed, but you can change it to your liking. The input will be validated to determine whether the certificate collection with this name already exists.

On top of that, you can select whether your certificate collection will be managed and tick the appropriate box for that. We will not do it this time. If you like to know what is going to happen when you tick the box, I invite you to check out the 2nd part of this blog post 😉 

Step 6. Depending on what the OpenID server provides, either the “Public Keys” or the “Certificates” Step will show up. 

It might also be possible that the step "Certificates" appears first, then the step "Public Keys". 

In our case, we only have “Public Keys” Step:

This step only comes up if there are public key definitions provided by the configuration that do not explicitly contain a certificate (which would then be displayed in step "Certificates”). 

The given names are validated so there are no empty name entries, no two names are the same, and no name of an already imported public key is entered. 

Review and creation of identity provider 

After you press the “review” button, all the information is checked.  

If this check finds an error in one or several steps due to invalid changes after a step was already left for its subsequent step, a message popover is displayed indicating which steps contain invalid input.  

You can navigate from these messages to the corresponding steps to correct the input.

After the final check is completed, all specified data is listed in the Review page. You still can edit the settings by pressing one of the step "Edit" buttons.

After successful execution, a summary page is displayed listing the performed actions: 

Deleting the identity provider 

In case you decide to delete JWT identity provider, you will have to options: restrict or cascade deletion. 

If you choose: 

  • “restrict”: only specified JWT identity provider will be deleted 
  • “cascade”: required only when the JWT provider is referenced by a Certification collection’s (or in short PSE's**) purpose object. The provider is removed from any PSE referencing it along with any user mappings for users authenticating with the provider. For PSEs where it is the only provider assigned, the purpose of that PSE is also removed.  

More about dropping JWT Provider you can read here.

Big thanks to the SAP HANA Development and Design team, without the help of whom this blog post would not have been possible! 

Additional resources:
